---
title: Linear Dependence
description: When a set of vectors has a non-trivial combination equal to zero — and
  when one is in the span of the others.
categories:
- Linear Algebra
---

A subset $A \subseteq V$ is **linearly dependent** if there exist distinct $\mathbf{v}_1, \dots, \mathbf{v}_n \in A$ and scalars $a_1, \dots, a_n \in \mathbb{R}$, **not all zero**, with $a_1\mathbf{v}_1 + \cdots + a_n\mathbf{v}_n = \mathbf{0}$. It is **linearly independent** if $a_1\mathbf{v}_1 + \cdots + a_n\mathbf{v}_n = \mathbf{0}$ forces $a_1 = \cdots = a_n = 0$.

**Examples.**

- $\{(1,2), (2,1), (1,1)\} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ is dependent: $(1,2) + (2,1) - 3(1,1) = \mathbf{0}$.
- $\varnothing$ is independent (there are no coefficients to be nonzero).
- If $\mathbf{0} \in A$, then $A$ is dependent, since $1 \cdot \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{0}$.
- If $\mathbf{v} = a\mathbf{w}$ for some $a$, then $1 \cdot \mathbf{v} - a\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$, so $\{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}\}$ is dependent.

**Singletons.** $\{\mathbf{v}\}$ is independent iff $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$.

**Theorem.** $A \cup \{\mathbf{v}\}$ is linearly dependent iff $\mathbf{v} \in \operatorname{span}(A \setminus \{\mathbf{v}\})$ — i.e. a set is dependent exactly when one of its vectors is a linear combination of the others. (Forward: if $\sum a_i \mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{0}$ with some $a_i \neq 0$, solve for $\mathbf{v}_i$. Backward: $\mathbf{v} = \sum a_j \mathbf{w}_j$ gives $\mathbf{0} = (-1)\mathbf{v} + \sum a_j \mathbf{w}_j$, a non-trivial combination.)

**Corollary.** If $\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{v}_j$ for some $i \neq j$, the list is dependent, since $1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_i - 1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_j = \mathbf{0}$.

**Example.** Are $(1,4,-1,3)$, $(2,1,1,0)$, $(-1,1,2,3) \in \mathbb{R}^4$ dependent? Row-reduce the matrix whose columns are these vectors:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 4 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \xrightarrow{\text{GE}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The only solution to $a_1\mathbf{v}_1 + a_2\mathbf{v}_2 + a_3\mathbf{v}_3 = \mathbf{0}$ is $a_1 = a_2 = a_3 = 0$, so the three vectors are **independent**.
